text:
DIAGNOSTIC IMAGING REPORT

EXAMINATION: MRI Lumbar Spine without and with Intravenous Gadolinium Contrast

CLINICAL INDICATION: 64-year-old female with known stage IV clear cell renal cell carcinoma (right kidney, status post cytoreductive right radical nephrectomy), with bilateral pulmonary metastatic disease currently on cabozantinib 40 mg daily (second-line therapy, approximately 9 months of treatment). New complaint of low back pain over the past 4 weeks, Grade 1-2 in severity. No focal neurological deficits on clinical examination. Evaluate for osseous metastatic disease versus underlying degenerative/structural etiology. History of incidentally noted mild lumbar spondylosis on prior cross-sectional imaging.

COMPARISON: Prior CT abdomen and pelvis with contrast (most recent restaging examination, performed approximately 3 months prior). MRI lumbar spine: no prior dedicated MRI lumbar spine available for direct comparison.

TECHNIQUE: Multiplanar, multisequence MRI of the lumbar spine was performed on a 1.5-Tesla scanner. Sequences obtained: sagittal T1-weighted spin echo, sagittal T2-weighted fast spin echo with fat saturation, sagittal STIR (short tau inversion recovery), axial T1-weighted spin echo through each disc level, axial T2-weighted fast spin echo, and post-contrast sagittal and axial T1-weighted fat-saturated sequences following administration of 0.1 mmol/kg of gadobutrol intravenously. No adverse reaction to contrast agent. Images are of diagnostic quality.

---

FINDINGS:

VERTEBRAL BODIES AND ALIGNMENT:
There are five non-rib-bearing lumbar vertebrae with normal numbering and configuration. Vertebral body heights are maintained throughout the lumbar spine, with no evidence of acute compression fracture or pathological height loss at any level. Vertebral body marrow signal is homogeneous and unremarkable on all sequences, with no focal areas of T1 hypointensity, T2 hyperintensity, or abnormal post-contrast enhancement that would suggest osseous metastatic infiltration. The STIR sequence, which is particularly sensitive for marrow edema and infiltrative processes, is negative for any focal marrow signal abnormality. Specifically, no lytic or sclerotic vertebral lesions are identified. The cortical margins of all lumbar vertebral bodies are intact. Mild loss of vertebral body height at L4 and L5 is noted, in keeping with longstanding degenerative change rather than acute pathology.

Alignment: There is mild lumbar lordosis, preserved and within normal limits. No spondylolisthesis or retrolisthesis identified.

INTERVERTEBRAL DISCS:
L1-L2: Mild disc desiccation (T2 hypointense disc) with minimal posterior disc bulge. No significant canal or foraminal compromise.

L2-L3: Mild disc desiccation. Small posterior disc bulge without nerve root impingement. No significant foraminal narrowing.

L3-L4: Moderate disc desiccation with mild diffuse disc bulge. Mild bilateral facet joint hypertrophy. No significant central canal stenosis. Mild bilateral foraminal narrowing, left greater than right, without definitive nerve root compression.

L4-L5: This level demonstrates the most advanced degenerative changes. There is moderate to marked disc desiccation with moderate loss of disc space height. A broad-based posterior disc bulge is present, superimposed on bilateral hypertrophic facet arthropathy with ligamentum flavum hypertrophy, resulting in mild-to-moderate central canal narrowing. Moderate bilateral foraminal narrowing is identified, more pronounced on the right side, secondary to the combined effect of disc bulge, hypertrophic facet changes, and uncinate hypertrophy. No definitive exiting nerve root compression is identified, though the right L4-L5 neural foramen is moderately narrowed with perineural fat effacement. No epidural mass or abnormal epidural enhancement. The findings at this level are chronic in nature.

L5-S1: Mild disc desiccation with small posterior disc bulge. Mild bilateral facet arthropathy. No significant central canal stenosis. Mild right foraminal narrowing without nerve root compression.

FACET JOINTS AND POSTERIOR ELEMENTS:
Bilateral facet arthropathy is present throughout the lumbar spine, most advanced at L4-L5 as described above. There is associated mild to moderate articular cartilage loss and subchondral sclerosis at the L3-L4 and L4-L5 facet joints bilaterally. No facet joint erosion, synovitis with abnormal enhancement, or epidural extension of any degenerative cyst. Posterior spinous processes and laminae are intact bilaterally. No spinous process or pedicle signal abnormality.

SPINAL CANAL AND NEURAL ELEMENTS:
The conus medullaris terminates at the L1-L2 level, which is a normal position. The conus is of normal morphology and signal intensity without intrinsic T2 signal abnormality. The cauda equina nerve roots are discretely visualized without focal thickening, abnormal clumping, or leptomeningeal enhancement on post-contrast sequences. There is no epidural collection (hematoma, abscess, or tumor). No evidence of spinal cord or cauda equina compression at any level. No intradural or extramedullary mass lesions. No meningeal enhancement or leptomeningeal carcinomatosis.

PARASPINAL SOFT TISSUES:
The paraspinal musculature demonstrates mild symmetric atrophy, particularly involving the multifidus and erector spinae groups bilaterally, a non-specific finding consistent with chronic deconditioning and age-related change. There is no focal paraspinal mass, soft tissue infiltration, or abnormal enhancement. No retroperitoneal lymphadenopathy is appreciated on the limited retroperitoneal views included in this examination. The partially visualized psoas muscles appear symmetric without abnormality.

SACRUM AND ILIAC CRESTS (PARTIALLY VISUALIZED):
The sacral body and sacral alar regions are partially visualized on this examination. The imaged portions of the sacrum demonstrate normal marrow signal without focal signal abnormality, lytic lesion, or abnormal enhancement. No sacral insufficiency fracture or focal destructive lesion identified. The partially visualized posterior iliac wings are within normal limits.

POST-CONTRAST SEQUENCES:
Following gadolinium administration, there is no pathological enhancement within the vertebral bodies, disc spaces, spinal canal, or paraspinal soft tissues. No epidural enhancement. No intradural or leptomeningeal enhancement. The facet joints demonstrate mildly increased enhancement in keeping with degenerative change; this is a non-specific finding and not suggestive of infectious or neoplastic involvement. No focal enhancing osseous lesion to suggest skeletal metastasis.

---

IMPRESSION:

1. No evidence of osseous metastatic disease within the imaged lumbar spine. No lytic or sclerotic vertebral lesions, no pathological vertebral fractures, no epidural tumor extension, and no abnormal marrow enhancement. The STIR sequences are negative for marrow infiltration at all levels.

2. Multilevel lumbar degenerative disc disease and facet arthropathy, most pronounced at L4-L5. Findings at L4-L5 include moderate disc height loss, broad-based posterior disc bulge, bilateral hypertrophic facet arthropathy with ligamentum flavum hypertrophy contributing to mild-to-moderate central canal narrowing, and moderate bilateral foraminal narrowing. These degenerative changes are chronic in appearance and are the most likely structural explanation for the patient's low back pain symptoms.

3. Mild multilevel degenerative changes at L1-L2 through L3-L4 and L5-S1, as described above, without significant neural compromise.

4. No intradural mass, leptomeningeal enhancement, epidural collection, or evidence of cauda equina compromise.

5. Conus medullaris terminates at L1-L2, normal in position and signal.

Overall, the low back pain in this patient with known metastatic renal cell carcinoma appears attributable to pre-existing multilevel lumbar degenerative disc disease and facet arthropathy rather than osseous metastatic involvement. Clinical and radiographic correlation is advised as clinically appropriate.

Signing Radiologist: Attending, Musculoskeletal and Neuroradiology
Division of Diagnostic Radiology
